column1: #   command: literal tool_input.command. The marker __NL__ expands to LF
#            (used by heredoc cases). Backslash sequences like \n are passed
#            through unchanged so printf "label: foo\n" stays literal.
#   env    : optional `KEY=VAL` env-var prefix (single segment, no spaces inside value)
# Lines beginning with `#` are skipped. Blank lines skipped. Tabs separate columns.
# Trailing column may be empty (no env).
# Regression for the gap where `rm -rf $HOME` (no subpath) was ALLOWED because
# HOME is whitelisted. Whitelist only certifies the var is shell-typed, not
# that the target is bounded — bare `$HOME` rms the user's entire home,
# and `$HOME/` rms `/` if HOME is somehow unset (Valve/steam-for-linux#3671:
# Real-world FPs from /cso 2026-04-30 (4 reproductions) — see CHANGELOG v0.5.5.

column3: cat <<EOF__NL__some prose mentioning npx pkg here__NL__EOF

column2: heredoc with quoted tag + npx in body

column1: # === multi-line bash: pattern extraction must not bleed across lines (v0.17.2 dogfood) ===
# operated per-line; lines without rm/npx passed through unchanged and either:
#   - false-ALLOW: rm-rf on line 2 → head -n1 returns line 1 (no rm content) →
#     rm_target empty → deny path never fires. §8 SAFETY bypass.
#   - false-DENY: npx-pin on line 2 → npx_tail starts with line 1's first token
#     (e.g. `TMP=$(mktemp`) → flagged as unpinned package. Innocent denied.
# Fix: SANITIZED_CMD_FLAT collapses `\n` → ` ` once, before both extraction passes.

column2: multi-line npx pinned on line 2 (was false-DENY)
multi-line npx local path on line 2
multi-line npx unpinned on line 2 (real catch)

column1: # === sanitize state-machine fix (silent §8 bypass via adjacent $-quoted regions) ===
# Pre-fix `sed -E 's/"[^"$]*"/""/g'` could pair the closing `"` of one
# $-containing string with the opening `"` of the next, eating `&& rm -rf`
# (or any other danger) between them. Repro: `echo "$A" && rm -rf "$B"`
# was sanitized to `echo "$A""$B"` and the rm-detector saw no `rm` at all.
# Fix: state-machine pairing in sanitize_cmd. The juxtaposition form
# `echo "$A" rm -rf "$B"` (no operator) is allowed by the readonly fast-path
# — bash-semantically it's just `echo` with 4 literal args, no rm execution.

column2: npx unpinned after && between two $-quoted strings
npx unpinned scoped after && between two $-quoted strings

column1: # === v0.21.4: rm-flag coverage extension + per-segment iteration ===

# at the LAST rm-rf in a multi-rm chain — earlier unguarded rm-rfs were
# silently skipped. Fix: per-segment iteration (split on `;`/`&&`/`||`/`|`/`&`/

# F1 (rm). SEC-1 (v0.39.0) basenamed only the rm gate; \npx / /usr/bin/npx and

column1: # F6: adjacent $IFS chains — the single-pass fold consumed the separator into the
# backref, leaving a following adjacent $IFS unfolded. Fold to a fixed point.
# === §8 FN closure F7 (2026-07-13 comprehensive audit, roadmap SEC-3) ===
# F7: command grouping / substitution EXECUTES — a cmd inside $(...) (...) {...;}
# or backtick runs at eval time; the rm splitter now breaks on ( ) backtick (NOT
# {} which is brace-VAR far more often) + strips a leading group opener, and the
# curl sink tolerates a closing ) / }. npx $(...) closed via the ( { anchor.
# === §8 FP closure F8 (2026-07-13 comprehensive audit, roadmap SEC-4) ===
# F8: mktemp-provenance — a var assigned THIS command from $(mktemp) / `mktemp`
# is a validated rm target (fresh unique path, §8.V4 disposal idiom). Cross-call
# (mktemp elsewhere), fake mktemp, and transitive vars stay strict.

column1: # === 2026-07-15 §8 FN matrix: assignment-value slash × quote-context pairing ===
# Two silent bypasses found by probe, both invisible in normal use (an ALLOW
# prints nothing, so neither could ever be user-reported).
# F10 — canon_cmd_words basenamed leading env-ASSIGNMENTS, so a slash in the
# VALUE deleted the assignment: `DEBUG=/tmp/x rm -rf $X` canon'd to `x rm -rf $X`
# → strip loop broke at `x` → rm_canon != rm → segment skipped → ALLOW. The
# pre-existing corpus rows (FOO=bar rm / FOO=bar npx, lines ~182/297) all use
# slash-FREE values, which is exactly why this survived SEC-2. Slash-bearing
# value + rm/npx in the SAME segment is the uncovered shape; both are locked here.

# echo "don't"` → ALLOW. Both quote types now share one state machine, so a `'`
# inside "..." is a literal and a `"` inside '...' is a literal.

column1: # === 2026-07-15 F16: rm/npx danger inside a same-line control structure (v0.48.0) ===
# PRE-EXISTING since per-segment iteration landed in v0.21.4. Segments split on `;`, so
# `if true; then rm -rf $X; fi` yields the segment `then rm -rf $X` — first word `then`,
# rm_canon != rm, segment skipped entirely. Every §8 rm/npx danger inside a same-line
# if/for/while was unguarded, including `if [ -d "$X" ]; then rm -rf "$X"; fi` — the most
# ordinary cleanup idiom in shell. The newline-separated form always denied (the keyword
# lands on its own segment); only the same-line form slipped. Fixed by treating do/then/
# else/! as reserved words in the wrapper-strip loop of both gates.

column3: for x in a; do rm -rf $DANGEROUS; done
if false; then :; else rm -rf $DANGEROUS; fi
if true; then npx unknown-pkg-xyz9; fi
for x in a; do npx unknown-pkg-xyz9; done
if true; then sudo -E rm -rf $DANGEROUS; fi
if true; then DEBUG=/tmp/x rm -rf $DANGEROUS; fi

column2: F16-fp: literal rm target inside then
F16-fp: reserved words as echo prose
column3: if true; then echo "do then else"; fi

column1: # Provenance rests on "VAR's value is determinable from the command text". That holds only
# if nothing else rebinds VAR. Enumerating rebind SYNTAX is a denylist that cannot be
# completed — probing found 7 forms invisible to the `VAR=` scan, all of which ALLOWed on
# v0.46.0-v0.47.2 (the mktemp recognizer had the same hole; this closes both kinds).
# Inverted: every rebind must NAME the var, so count bare (non-`$`) mentions and require
# the count to EQUAL the number of assignments actually classified. Surplus = unseen
# rebind. `source`/`.` is the one residual the count cannot see (rebinds from another
# file without naming the var here), so it is rejected outright.

column2: F17: unset after a mktemp assignment
F17: for-loop rebinds the target var
column3: S=$(mktemp -d); for S in $EVIL; do rm -rf "$S"; done

column1: # === D1 (2026-07-15): canon_cmd_words must not treat ${VAR} brace as a

# the `${SP}` the var-detector greps for -> silent bypass. `rm -rf "${HOME}/"`
# (empty HOME = rm -rf /, the steam-for-linux#3671 residue case) bypassed too.

column1: # in_heredoc, truncating the line at `<<` and blanking following lines -> the
# rm/npx/curl after the arithmetic was deleted from the text ALL three detectors
# scan -> silent bypass of every gate. Arithmetic spans are masked before the
# heredoc test; a real heredoc whose body contains arithmetic is unaffected.

column2: D2: $((1<<b)) blinds the npx gate too
D2-fp: real heredoc, arithmetic in body

column1: # IFS as a leading assignment does not hide the rm target (reviewer NOT-PROBED
# item, probed 2026-07-15): `IFS=/` is stripped as a leading env-assignment, so
# `$SP` is still detected. A crafted IFS routed elsewhere is deliberate-bypass
# territory (same class as the indirect-name rebind residual documented in the
# hook) and is not chased -- it does not clear the "ordinary mistake" bar.
# === 2026-07-15 fresh-review: the D2 class is broader than $(( )). heredoc_re
# matched `<<word` in ANY context, so a left-shift in $[a<<b] / `let a<<b`, a
# `<<` inside a quoted string, or `<<` in comparison prose all faked a heredoc
# and blanked the following rm/npx/curl. Root-cause fix: only treat `<<TAG` as a
# heredoc when a matching terminator line exists (a real heredoc always closes;
# a shift / quoted `<<` / comparison never does).

column2: rev1: let a<<b builtin-arith shift then rm
rev2: << inside double-quoted string then rm
rev2: << inside single-quoted string then rm
rev-fp: real heredoc body containing rm (data)
